name: Sraosha
subtitle: Yazata of Obedience, Divine Word, and Guardian of Souls
description: Sraosha (Avestan: 'Hearkening' or 'Obedience') stands as one of the most important yazatas (adorable ones) in Zoroastrian theology, embodying the sacred concept of obedient listening to divine revelation and the disciplined observance of religious duties. As the heavenly messenger who bears prayers from earth to heaven, Sraosha functions much like the archangels of Abrahamic traditions. He guards the souls of the righteous during the dangerous three-day journey after death, protecting them from demonic assault as they approach the Chinvat Bridge of judgment. Known as the 'Warrior Yazata,' Sraosha actively battles the forces of evil, particularly the demon Aeshma (fury/wrath), his eternal adversary. His rooster's crow at dawn drives away the demons of the night, making the rooster his sacred animal. Sraosha represents the active, living religion - the divine word heard, understood, and practiced through righteous action.

sections.Origins: Sraosha's origins lie in the Indo-Iranian religious substrate shared with Vedic tradition. His name derives from the Avestan root 'sru-' meaning 'to hear,' cognate with Sanskrit 'shru.' In the earliest Zoroastrian texts, the Gathas of Zarathustra, Sraosha appears as a personification of religious discipline and the obedient hearing of Ahura Mazda's wisdom. He was already among the most venerated yazatas in ancient Iranian religion, his cult surviving the Zoroastrian reformation and becoming central to later developments. The Yasna liturgy dedicates significant sections to Sraosha, and the entire 57th Yasht is devoted to his praise.
sections.Mythology: In Zoroastrian mythology, Sraosha performs several crucial functions. He is the divine messenger who carries prayers and sacrifices from worshippers to Ahura Mazda and the Amesha Spentas in heaven. During the three nights after death, when the soul lingers near the body before judgment, Sraosha stands guard against the demons who seek to claim it. He escorts the righteous soul safely to the Chinvat Bridge, where he participates in the judgment alongside Mithra and Rashnu. His eternal battle against Aeshma, the demon of wrath, represents the struggle between disciplined righteousness and chaotic fury. When the rooster crows at dawn, it is Sraosha's voice calling the faithful to prayer and driving back the creatures of darkness.
sections.Role: Sraosha occupies a unique position in the Zoroastrian divine hierarchy. While not one of the seven Amesha Spentas (the supreme divine beings closest to Ahura Mazda), he stands among the foremost yazatas, sometimes grouped with Mithra and Rashnu as the three divinities presiding over the judgment of souls. His role as guardian of the night makes him the special protector of sleeping humans, and prayers to him form part of the evening devotions. The Srosh Baj, a prayer invoking his protection, is recited during dangerous times and transitions. In temple ritual, he is closely associated with the sacred fire, another force that drives back darkness and evil.
sections.Legacy: Sraosha's influence extends beyond Zoroastrianism into the broader religious history of the Near East and Mediterranean. His role as heavenly messenger carrying prayers between humans and the divine closely parallels Gabriel in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. The Greek god Hermes in his role as psychopomp (soul-guide) shares functions with Sraosha, as does the Egyptian Thoth. The connection between Sraosha and the rooster persists in folk traditions across the Persian-influenced world, where the cock's crow remains associated with the dispersal of evil spirits. In modern Zoroastrian practice, Sraosha remains a beloved and frequently invoked yazata, his name appearing in daily prayers and protective invocations.

associations.name: Obedience
associations.significance: Sraosha embodies the principle of obedience to Ahura Mazda's divine law, a core tenet of Zoroastrianism.

associations.name: Discipline
associations.significance: Represents the disciplined adherence to religious duties and moral principles.

associations.name: Stars
associations.significance: Associated with the celestial realm and the guiding light of wisdom.

associations.name: Rooster
associations.significance: The rooster's crow symbolizes Sraosha's call to awaken and resist the forces of darkness before dawn.

associations.name: Prayer
associations.significance: As the messenger of prayers, Sraosha is intrinsically linked to the act of supplication and communication with the divine.

associations.name: Three
associations.significance: Associated with the three times Sraosha descends to earth each day to protect humanity.

worshipPractices.name: Prayer Recitation
worshipPractices.description: Devotees recite prayers, particularly the Yasna, to invoke Sraosha's protection and guidance. These prayers emphasize obedience to Ahura Mazda.

worshipPractices.name: Ritual Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of food and incense are made during Zoroastrian rituals to honor Sraosha and other yazatas. These offerings symbolize devotion and gratitude.

festivals.name: Sraosha's Day
festivals.timing: The 17th day of each month in the Zoroastrian calendar
festivals.description: Dedicated to Sraosha, this day involves special prayers and observances to honor his role as the guardian of obedience and protector of humanity.

cultural.modernLegacy: Sraosha's emphasis on obedience and discipline continues to influence Zoroastrian ethics and religious practice. He is seen as a model for moral conduct.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Sraosha's origins are deeply rooted in the ancient Indo-Iranian religious traditions that predate Zoroastrianism. Within the Zoroastrian scriptures, particularly the Avesta, Sraosha emerges as a divine being created by Ahura Mazda, the supreme god. His birth is not described in a literal, physical sense but rather as an emanation of Ahura Mazda's divine will and wisdom. He is often depicted as the first spiritual being to actively combat the forces of evil, particularly the daeva named Bushyasta, who embodies sloth and disobedience. Sraosha's role as a protector of humanity begins immediately after his creation, highlighting his integral importance in the cosmic struggle between good and evil. His very existence is a testament to the importance of heeding divine guidance and upholding moral order in the face of temptation and adversity.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: One of Sraosha's most prominent roles is as a guardian and protector of humanity, especially during the night. Zoroastrians believe that during sleep, the soul is vulnerable to the forces of evil. Sraosha, armed with his divine might, descends to earth three times each night to combat these dark forces, preventing them from harming sleeping individuals and disrupting the cosmic order. He is often depicted battling Bushyasta, the demon of sloth, ensuring that people wake up with the resolve to perform their religious duties. Another significant myth involves Sraosha's role as a judge of souls after death. He, along with Mithra and Rashnu, presides over the Chinvat Bridge, a spiritual bridge that souls must cross to reach paradise or hell. Sraosha's judgment is based on the individual's obedience to Ahura Mazda's laws and their adherence to moral principles throughout their lifetime.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Sraosha is venerated through prayer, ritual offerings, and adherence to Zoroastrian moral principles. Prayers dedicated to Sraosha are recited during various religious ceremonies, especially during the Yasna, the principal Zoroastrian act of worship. These prayers emphasize the importance of obedience, discipline, and vigilance against evil. Offerings of food, incense, and other symbolic items are made to honor Sraosha and seek his blessings. The 17th day of each month in the Zoroastrian calendar is dedicated to Sraosha, providing a special occasion for devotees to express their reverence and strengthen their commitment to his teachings. Zoroastrians strive to emulate Sraosha's virtues in their daily lives, practicing obedience to divine law, maintaining discipline in their actions, and remaining vigilant against the temptations of evil. This imitation of Sraosha's character is considered a vital aspect of worship.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Sraosha's legacy extends beyond the specific rituals and prayers dedicated to him. His embodiment of obedience, discipline, and vigilance has deeply influenced Zoroastrian ethics and moral philosophy. He serves as a constant reminder of the importance of adhering to Ahura Mazda's divine law and resisting the forces of evil. The concept of Sraosha has also resonated in other religious and philosophical traditions, inspiring notions of divine messengers, moral guardians, and the importance of spiritual discipline. Although Zoroastrianism is a relatively small religion today, Sraosha's enduring symbolism continues to inspire those seeking guidance and strength in their spiritual journeys. His image remains a powerful symbol of the ongoing struggle between good and evil, encouraging individuals to embrace righteousness and resist the allure of darkness. Sraosha's presence in Zoroastrianism underscores the faith's emphasis on personal responsibility and the pursuit of moral perfection.

shortDescription: Sraosha, in Persian mythology, embodies obedience to divine law and protects humanity from evil. He is a vital yazata and divine messenger.
